title: Ephemerantha quadriloba
intro: Ephemerantha quadriloba, also known as the "Queen of the Andes," is a flowering plant native to the high-altitude regions of South America. It has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, colds, and respiratory issues, due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which may also contribute to its potential to reduce oxidative stress and improve cardiovascular health. The bioactive constituents of E. quadriloba include flavonoids, saponins, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are valued for their ability to boost the immune system and alleviate symptoms associated with respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Ephemerantha quadriloba is classified as a species of flowering plant in the orchid family, Orchidaceae. More specifically, it is part of the subfamily Vanilloideae and is closely related to the genus Ponthieva. Ephemerantha quadriloba is also sometimes placed in the genus Ponthieva, but its exact taxonomic classification can vary depending on the source. The plant's classification is constantly being studied and revised, reflecting the ongoing efforts of botanists to understand the complex relationships within the orchid family.
